summary.brief: Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: The claimant's nephew was born and was subsequently diagnosed as having haemophilia.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The claimant wished to avoid having a child with that condition and so consulted a general practitioner, Dr Athukorala, in August 2006.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Blood tests were arranged. However, such tests were those to establish whether a patient had haemophilia and could not confirm whether or not the claimant was a carrier.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The claimant was told that the results were normal. As a result of the advice she received at that consultation and the previous consultation, she was led to believe that any child she had would not have haemophilia.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In December 2010, the claimant became pregnant with FGN. Shortly after his birth in September 2011 he was diagnosed as having haemophilia.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: FGN's haemophilia is severe. He has been unresponsive to conventional factor VIII replacement therapy.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: In December 2015, FGN was diagnosed as also suffering from autism.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Court of Appeal has decided in Parkinson and Groom that recovery for the costs associated with a disability not directly linked to the negligence is fair where the disabled child would not have been born but for the negligence.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: It follows that I consider that the costs related to FGN's autism may properly be recovered by the claimant from the defendant.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.chief_complaint: Consultation to determine carrier status for haemophilia
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: The claimant wished to avoid having a child with haemophilia and consulted a general practitioner to establish if she was a carrier.

focused_exam.system: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

critical_findings: Blood tests arranged were not suitable for determining carrier status

requestables.name: Genetic Testing for Haemophilia Carrier Status
requestables.description: Referral to a haematologist for genetic testing to determine carrier status

reveal.decision_impact: Would have led to informed decision-making regarding pregnancy

timeline_phases.name: Initial Consultation
timeline_phases.description: Patient consults GP to determine haemophilia carrier status

timeline_phases.name: Blood Test Results
timeline_phases.description: Patient receives results of blood tests

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient consulting to determine haemophilia carrier status
decision_points.prompt: What test should be ordered to determine if the patient is a carrier of the haemophilia gene?

options.description: Order genetic testing for haemophilia carrier status
options.clinical_reasoning: Genetic testing is required to determine carrier status accurately

options.description: Order blood tests to check for haemophilia
options.clinical_reasoning: Blood tests for haemophilia do not determine carrier status

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant ordered blood tests for haemophilia
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Defendant believed these tests were appropriate

expected_action_court.description: Genetic testing should have been ordered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established genetic testing is required

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Blood tests ordered could not determine carrier status
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Referral for genetic testing should have been made

patient_outcome.description: Child born with haemophilia and later diagnosed with autism
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Requires ongoing management for haemophilia and autism

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to refer for genetic testing
Causation established - child would not have been born but for negligence

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to order appropriate genetic testing
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Genetic testing would have shown carrier status, leading to different pregnancy decisions

factual_timeline.event: Patient consults GP to determine haemophilia carrier status

allegations.allegation: Failure to refer for genetic testing
